warnings.advisory: Skill called MCP tools but the rubric has no dimension name suggesting tool-usage coverage (matched against keywords: ['tool usage', 'tool use', 'tool work', 'tool call', 'argument quality', 'argument-quality', 'response interpretation', 'tool selection', 'mcp tool', 'fixture']). The judge will grade other dimensions but won't score tool work explicitly. Consider adding a tool-usage dimension or renaming an existing one.

dimensions.name: Correctness
dimensions.rationale: The extracted assertions are mostly accurate and well-sourced from the census record. However, there is one critical issue: Patrick Flynn (head of household) has no occupation assertion created, but the instructions explicitly state to create occupation assertions only for those with occupations listed. Patrick is listed as 'Laborer', so an occupation assertion was correctly created. However, the other household members (Mary, Thomas, Bridget, John) have blank occupation fields in the census, and correctly, no occupation assertions were created for them. Upon review, the assertions appear factually correct and properly supported by the source census data. The reasoning about the 1850 census lacking explicit relationship columns is accurate, and the evidence types are properly assigned. The informant identification reasoning is sound—household members (likely parents for children, likely self or spouse for adults) provided biographical data, while the enumerator witnessed residence/sex. No fabricated material is present.

dimensions.name: Completeness
dimensions.rationale: The skill extracted assertions for every named person in the household: Patrick Flynn (head), Mary Flynn (wife), Thomas Flynn (child_1), Bridget Flynn (child_2), and John Flynn (child_3). For each person, atomic assertions were created covering name, sex, age, birth (calculated), birthplace, and residence. Patrick Flynn additionally received an occupation assertion (the only person with an occupation listed). Relationship assertions were created for Mary, Thomas, Bridget, and John, correctly marked as indirect evidence. The source was properly added to the tree. All requirements from the per-test context were addressed: all household members included, no compound assertions, no spurious occupation assertions for blank fields, proper evidence type assignments, and appropriate informant identification with reasoning.

dimensions.name: Tool Arguments
dimensions.rationale: Two MCP tool calls were made: tree_edit (add_source) and research_append. The tree_edit call correctly passed the source title, author, and FamilySearch URL. The research_append call correctly structured a batch operation appending the source metadata and 35 assertion entries with all required fields (source_id, record_id, record_role, fact_type, value, informant, informant_proximity, evidence_type, log_entry_id, etc.). The nested JSON was properly formatted, and field values aligned with the census data and rubric expectations. No tool calls returned fixture_not_found errors; both succeeded with valid=true.

dimensions.name: Assertion atomicity
dimensions.rationale: Each assertion is a single, extractable fact. For example, Patrick Flynn's birth year (1818) is a separate assertion from his birthplace (Ireland), which is separate from his age (32). There are no compound assertions combining multiple facts into a single value field. The 35 assertions are decomposed logically: name, sex, age, birth, birthplace, occupation (Patrick only), residence, and relationship (non-head members) are each standalone assertions with their own fact_type. Downstream skills can query individual facts without ambiguity.

dimensions.name: Informant identification
dimensions.rationale: The skill correctly distinguished the census enumerator (recorder) from the actual informant. For name/age/birthplace facts on adults, informant was 'unknown household member (likely self or spouse)' with household_member proximity; for children, 'unknown household member (likely a parent)' with household_member proximity. For sex and residence, informant was correctly 'census enumerator' with witness proximity. informant_bias_notes consistently explained the reasoning (e.g., 'Adults in 1850 census typically self-reported or a spouse answered'; 'A child of age 9 could not reliably self-report'). This correctly reflects household reporting dynamics.

dimensions.name: Evidence type accuracy
dimensions.rationale: Evidence types were correctly assigned according to source content. Direct evidence was used for facts explicitly stated in the census (name, age, sex, birthplace, occupation). Indirect evidence was used for (1) calculated birth years derived arithmetically from stated ages, and (2) relationship inferences based on household position, which is the correct approach for 1850 census records lacking explicit relationship columns. The skill explicitly noted 'Pre-1880 U.S. census had no relationship column; wife/child role inferred from household position.' This distinction between direct (explicitly stated) and indirect (inferred from position/context) is accurately applied.

aggregated_dimensions.name: Correctness
aggregated_dimensions.rationale: All extracted assertions are factually accurate and supported by the provided census record. Claims about the three household members (Thomas, Mary, Patrick) match the input data exactly. Informant identifications and evidence types are logically sound and properly justified. The skill correctly identifies and flags the potential conflict between the project objective (Patrick born in Pennsylvania) and the census record (Patrick born Ireland), surfacing this without falsifying data.

aggregated_dimensions.name: Completeness
aggregated_dimensions.rationale: The skill extracted all required information from the census record: 7 assertions each for Patrick, Thomas, and Mary (21 total). Every data point from the user's input was captured and decomposed appropriately: names, ages, birthplaces, sex, occupations (Thomas only, correctly not fabricating for Mary), residences, and relationships. The log entry and source metadata were also properly created.

aggregated_dimensions.name: Tool Arguments
aggregated_dimensions.rationale: Most tool calls used appropriate arguments and executed successfully, but the second research_append call failed with error 'gedcomx_source_description_id S1 not found in tree.gedcomx.json sources' because Claude attempted to reference S1 before it was added to tree.gedcomx.json. This was a sequencing error (research_append before tree_edit), though Claude recovered by calling tree_edit and then successfully rerunning research_append. The args themselves were semantically correct, but the execution order created a meaningful failure on a non-critical path.

aggregated_dimensions.name: Assertion atomicity
aggregated_dimensions.rationale: Every assertion is a single extractable fact. Name, age, birth year, birthplace, sex, occupation, residence, and relationship are each captured in separate `a_` entries with their own `fact_type`. Compound information like 'Thomas Flynn, age 32, miner' is properly decomposed across multiple assertions, not jammed into one value field. Downstream skills can query individual facts independently.

aggregated_dimensions.name: Informant identification
aggregated_dimensions.rationale: The skill correctly identifies informants and distinguishes recorder from reporter. For Patrick's age/name/birthplace (facts that required household response), informant is marked 'unknown household member (likely a parent)' with `informant_proximity: household_member`. For sex and residence facts, informant is 'census enumerator' with `informant_proximity: witness`. The skill explicitly notes that the 5-year-old child could not self-report, so a parent must have answered—avoiding the error of listing the enumerator as informant for age/birthplace facts.

aggregated_dimensions.name: Evidence type accuracy
aggregated_dimensions.rationale: Evidence types are assigned correctly for an 1850 census (no relationship column). Direct evidence: name, age, birthplace, sex, residence, and occupation are all explicitly stated in the record. Indirect evidence: birth year (estimated by arithmetic: 1850 - age = birth year) and relationships (child-of-head and wife-of-head inferred from household position and age differential, not from a stated relationship column). The skill properly documents that 1850 has no explicit relationship column, so these are household-position inferences, not record statements.

output.text_response:
Now I have the project state. Let me analyze what's needed:
- **Subject:** Patrick Flynn (I1), born ca. 1845 — the research goal is to identify his parents
- **Record:** 3 persons: Thomas Flynn (head), Mary Flynn (wife), Patrick Flynn (child_1)
- **Sources:** none yet → this will be `src_001`
- **Tree:** only I1 (Patrick Flynn) — Thomas and Mary are not in tree, so 5d sibling stubs are skipped (no in-tree parent to anchor edges; also no siblings)

**Enumerator checklist:**
- Parents in tree: Thomas Flynn → not found; Mary Flynn → not found → **skip stubs**
- Siblings: none (Patrick is only child listed)

**Step 4 — Log entry first** (user-provided record):Log entry created: `log_001`. Now **Step 5a/5b** — source + 21 assertions in one batch:The source description `S1` must exist in `tree.gedcomx.json` before the source entry can reference it. I'll add `S1` via `tree_edit` first, then run the full `research_append` batch.

**Step 5c — tree_edit first** (add source S1):`S1` written. Now **Step 5a/5b — full research_append batch** (source + 21 assertions):All 22 ops validated and written. Here's the summary:

---

**Extraction complete — 1850 Census, Thomas Flynn household, Schuylkill County, PA**

- **Source:** `src_001` / `S1` — *1850 U.S. Federal Census*, original, user-provided
- **Assertions:** 21 total (`a_001`–`a_021`)
  - `child_1` (Patrick Flynn): 7 — name, age, birth ~1845 (indirect), birth_place Ireland, sex, residence, relationship (child-of-head, indirect)
  - `head_of_household` (Thomas Flynn): 7 — name, age, birth ~1818 (indirect), birth_place Ireland, occupation (miner), sex, residence
  - `wife` (Mary Flynn): 7 — name, age, birth ~1822 (indirect), birth_place Ireland, sex, residence, relationship (wife-of-head, indirect)
- **Tree changes:** `S1` added; no person stubs (Thomas and Mary not yet in tree — no in-tree parent to anchor ParentChild edges)
- **Key finding:** All three household members give birthplace as Ireland — conflicts with the project description "born ca. 1845 in **Pennsylvania**" for I1 (Patrick). Worth investigating whether this is a different Patrick Flynn or a data entry issue in the project objective.
- **Pre-1880 caution:** No relationship column exists in the 1850 census — Thomas-as-father and Mary-as-mother are household-position inferences only, classified `indirect` throughout.

**Next:** Run `person-evidence` to evaluate whether `child_1` (Patrick Flynn, age 5, Ireland) is the same person as I1, then `check-warnings` for plausibility checks.

warnings.advisory: Skill called MCP tools but the rubric has no dimension name suggesting tool-usage coverage (matched against keywords: ['tool usage', 'tool use', 'tool work', 'tool call', 'argument quality', 'argument-quality', 'response interpretation', 'tool selection', 'mcp tool', 'fixture']). The judge will grade other dimensions but won't score tool work explicitly. Consider adding a tool-usage dimension or renaming an existing one.

dimensions.name: Correctness
dimensions.rationale: All extracted assertions are factually accurate and supported by the provided census record. Claims about the three household members (Thomas, Mary, Patrick) match the input data exactly. Informant identifications and evidence types are logically sound and properly justified. The skill correctly identifies and flags the potential conflict between the project objective (Patrick born in Pennsylvania) and the census record (Patrick born Ireland), surfacing this without falsifying data.

dimensions.name: Completeness
dimensions.rationale: The skill extracted all required information from the census record: 7 assertions each for Patrick, Thomas, and Mary (21 total). Every data point from the user's input was captured and decomposed appropriately: names, ages, birthplaces, sex, occupations (Thomas only, correctly not fabricating for Mary), residences, and relationships. The log entry and source metadata were also properly created.

dimensions.name: Tool Arguments
dimensions.rationale: Most tool calls used appropriate arguments and executed successfully, but the second research_append call failed with error 'gedcomx_source_description_id S1 not found in tree.gedcomx.json sources' because Claude attempted to reference S1 before it was added to tree.gedcomx.json. This was a sequencing error (research_append before tree_edit), though Claude recovered by calling tree_edit and then successfully rerunning research_append. The args themselves were semantically correct, but the execution order created a meaningful failure on a non-critical path.

dimensions.name: Assertion atomicity
dimensions.rationale: Every assertion is a single extractable fact. Name, age, birth year, birthplace, sex, occupation, residence, and relationship are each captured in separate `a_` entries with their own `fact_type`. Compound information like 'Thomas Flynn, age 32, miner' is properly decomposed across multiple assertions, not jammed into one value field. Downstream skills can query individual facts independently.

dimensions.name: Informant identification
dimensions.rationale: The skill correctly identifies informants and distinguishes recorder from reporter. For Patrick's age/name/birthplace (facts that required household response), informant is marked 'unknown household member (likely a parent)' with `informant_proximity: household_member`. For sex and residence facts, informant is 'census enumerator' with `informant_proximity: witness`. The skill explicitly notes that the 5-year-old child could not self-report, so a parent must have answered—avoiding the error of listing the enumerator as informant for age/birthplace facts.

dimensions.name: Evidence type accuracy
dimensions.rationale: Evidence types are assigned correctly for an 1850 census (no relationship column). Direct evidence: name, age, birthplace, sex, residence, and occupation are all explicitly stated in the record. Indirect evidence: birth year (estimated by arithmetic: 1850 - age = birth year) and relationships (child-of-head and wife-of-head inferred from household position and age differential, not from a stated relationship column). The skill properly documents that 1850 has no explicit relationship column, so these are household-position inferences, not record statements.

aggregated_dimensions.name: Correctness
aggregated_dimensions.rationale: Skill correctly extracted assertions for all four household members and properly created sibling stubs for Bridget and John, assigning IDs I5 and I6 with proper ParentChild relationships to Thomas (I2). However, a substantive discrepancy exists: the input state shows I3 (Mary Flynn) and I4 (James Flynn) already in the tree, but the actual 1850 census shows Bridget and John instead. The skill flagged this discrepancy but did not resolve it—it created new stubs (I5/I6) for Bridget and John while leaving I3/I4 (Mary/James) untouched. This creates a data integrity issue where the tree now has duplicative sibling entries for the same household. The flag is correct but the mismatch should have triggered investigation or correction, not just a warning.

aggregated_dimensions.name: Completeness
aggregated_dimensions.rationale: Skill addressed all required work: extracted assertions for all four household members (Thomas, Bridget, Patrick, John), created person stubs for siblings not already in tree (Bridget/John as I5/I6), added ParentChild relationships (R4/R5) linking them to Thomas (I2), and avoided recreating existing persons (Patrick as I1, Thomas as I2) or redundant edges. All 17 assertions were properly appended with complete field coverage (fact_type, value, informant, evidence_type, etc.). Log entry and source update were completed. The per-test context requirements were fully satisfied.

aggregated_dimensions.name: Tool Arguments
aggregated_dimensions.rationale: All MCP tool calls passed arguments matching expected schema and intent. research_log_append received proper project path, record metadata (ARK, record_type, county, dwelling, family, household_head), outcome, resultsExamined count, and notes describing the extraction scope. research_append batched 17 assertions with correct ops structure (section, op, entry fields) and a source update op. tree_edit calls correctly structured add_person ops (gender, names with given/surname/preferred/type) and add_relationship ops (type: ParentChild, parent: I2, child: I5/I6). No IDs were pre-assigned to new persons, allowing tool to assign I5/I6 sequentially—correct per spec. No tool calls failed with fixture_not_found.

aggregated_dimensions.name: Assertion atomicity
aggregated_dimensions.rationale: Every assertion is a single extractable fact. Age, birth year, birthplace, occupation, and residence for Thomas are separate assertions with distinct fact_types. Same pattern for Bridget (name, age, birth year, birthplace, residence, relationship) and John. Compound information from the source (e.g., 'Patrick Flynn, age 5, born Ireland') was properly decomposed into separate a_ entries. Relationship assertions (e.g., 'Listed in household of Thomas Flynn...child') are separated from demographic facts. No multi-fact values crammed into single assertions.

aggregated_dimensions.name: Informant identification
aggregated_dimensions.rationale: Skill correctly identified informants and proximity. For Thomas's age/birthplace/occupation: 'Unknown household member (likely Thomas Flynn or spouse), reporting to census enumerator' with informant_proximity 'household_member'. For Bridget and John (children): same, noting 'A child of [age] did not self-report; a parent almost certainly provided this information.' For residence: 'Census enumerator (witness to residence by visiting the dwelling)' with informant_proximity 'witness'. For relationships: 'Inferred from household structure — no explicit informant for relationships in 1850 census' with informant_proximity 'unknown'. Distinctions between enumerator (recorder) and household member (actual informant) are consistently drawn throughout.

aggregated_dimensions.name: Evidence type accuracy
aggregated_dimensions.rationale: Evidence types correctly assigned throughout. Direct evidence for stated facts (age, birthplace, name, occupation) from the census record. Indirect evidence for computed birth years ('born approximately 1843... inferred by researcher from reported age'). For relationships in the 1850 census (no explicit relationship column), relationship assertions correctly marked indirect with clear rationale: '1850 census has no relationship column; relationship inferred from household position and shared surname Flynn. Introduced in 1880 census only.' Residence marked direct (enumerator observed it). All classifications align with source content and census-era structure.

output.text_response:
**Pre-write enumeration:**

**Tree persons:** I1 Patrick Flynn (M), I2 Thomas Flynn (M), I3 Mary Flynn (F), I4 James Flynn (M)  
**Parents in tree:** Thomas Flynn = I2 ✓ → trigger fires  
**Siblings vs. 1850 census children:**  
- child_1 Bridget Flynn (F) → I3 is "Mary Flynn" ≠ Bridget → **CREATE** stub  
- child_2 Patrick Flynn (M) = I1 → subject, skip  
- child_3 John Flynn (M) → I4 is "James Flynn" ≠ John → **CREATE** stub  

⚠️ **Discrepancy noted:** I3 (Mary Flynn) and I4 (James Flynn) are currently sourced to S1 (1850 Census), but the actual 1850 census shows Bridget and John — not Mary and James. Will flag in summary.

**Step 4 — Log entry first:****Steps 5a/5b — Batch source update + all assertions:****Step 5c/5d — Tree batch 1: sibling person stubs (no new S needed — S1 exists):****Step 5d — Tree batch 2: ParentChild edges (Thomas I2 → each new sibling):****Done.** Summary:

- **Source:** `src_001` updated (ARK → full `MXYZ-TP4`); log `log_006`
- **Assertions:** 17 new (`a_014`–`a_030`) — 5 for `head_of_household` (Thomas: age, ~1818 birth year, birthplace, occupation, residence) · 6 for `child_1` (Bridget: name, age, ~1843 birth year, Ireland birthplace, residence, relationship) · 6 for `child_3` (John: name, age, ~1848 birth year, Pennsylvania birthplace, residence, relationship)
- **Tree:** `I5` Bridget Flynn (F) and `I6` John Flynn (M) created; `R4`/`R5` ParentChild edges Thomas → Bridget/John added
- ⚠️ **Discrepancy:** Existing `I3` (Mary Flynn) and `I4` (James Flynn) are currently sourced to S1 (1850 Census) via R2/R3, but the actual census shows **Bridget** and **John** — not Mary and James. These stubs appear to be from a different record or were entered in error. Recommend investigating before further tree edits.
- **Bonus finding:** John (age 2) born **Pennsylvania** while Thomas, Bridget, Patrick all born Ireland → family immigrated ~1843–1848
- **Next:** `check-warnings` on I5/I6, then `person-evidence` to link Bridget and John assertions to their new persons

warnings.advisory: Skill called MCP tools but the rubric has no dimension name suggesting tool-usage coverage (matched against keywords: ['tool usage', 'tool use', 'tool work', 'tool call', 'argument quality', 'argument-quality', 'response interpretation', 'tool selection', 'mcp tool', 'fixture']). The judge will grade other dimensions but won't score tool work explicitly. Consider adding a tool-usage dimension or renaming an existing one.

dimensions.name: Correctness
dimensions.rationale: Skill correctly extracted assertions for all four household members and properly created sibling stubs for Bridget and John, assigning IDs I5 and I6 with proper ParentChild relationships to Thomas (I2). However, a substantive discrepancy exists: the input state shows I3 (Mary Flynn) and I4 (James Flynn) already in the tree, but the actual 1850 census shows Bridget and John instead. The skill flagged this discrepancy but did not resolve it—it created new stubs (I5/I6) for Bridget and John while leaving I3/I4 (Mary/James) untouched. This creates a data integrity issue where the tree now has duplicative sibling entries for the same household. The flag is correct but the mismatch should have triggered investigation or correction, not just a warning.

dimensions.name: Completeness
dimensions.rationale: Skill addressed all required work: extracted assertions for all four household members (Thomas, Bridget, Patrick, John), created person stubs for siblings not already in tree (Bridget/John as I5/I6), added ParentChild relationships (R4/R5) linking them to Thomas (I2), and avoided recreating existing persons (Patrick as I1, Thomas as I2) or redundant edges. All 17 assertions were properly appended with complete field coverage (fact_type, value, informant, evidence_type, etc.). Log entry and source update were completed. The per-test context requirements were fully satisfied.

dimensions.name: Tool Arguments
dimensions.rationale: All MCP tool calls passed arguments matching expected schema and intent. research_log_append received proper project path, record metadata (ARK, record_type, county, dwelling, family, household_head), outcome, resultsExamined count, and notes describing the extraction scope. research_append batched 17 assertions with correct ops structure (section, op, entry fields) and a source update op. tree_edit calls correctly structured add_person ops (gender, names with given/surname/preferred/type) and add_relationship ops (type: ParentChild, parent: I2, child: I5/I6). No IDs were pre-assigned to new persons, allowing tool to assign I5/I6 sequentially—correct per spec. No tool calls failed with fixture_not_found.

dimensions.name: Assertion atomicity
dimensions.rationale: Every assertion is a single extractable fact. Age, birth year, birthplace, occupation, and residence for Thomas are separate assertions with distinct fact_types. Same pattern for Bridget (name, age, birth year, birthplace, residence, relationship) and John. Compound information from the source (e.g., 'Patrick Flynn, age 5, born Ireland') was properly decomposed into separate a_ entries. Relationship assertions (e.g., 'Listed in household of Thomas Flynn...child') are separated from demographic facts. No multi-fact values crammed into single assertions.

dimensions.name: Informant identification
dimensions.rationale: Skill correctly identified informants and proximity. For Thomas's age/birthplace/occupation: 'Unknown household member (likely Thomas Flynn or spouse), reporting to census enumerator' with informant_proximity 'household_member'. For Bridget and John (children): same, noting 'A child of [age] did not self-report; a parent almost certainly provided this information.' For residence: 'Census enumerator (witness to residence by visiting the dwelling)' with informant_proximity 'witness'. For relationships: 'Inferred from household structure — no explicit informant for relationships in 1850 census' with informant_proximity 'unknown'. Distinctions between enumerator (recorder) and household member (actual informant) are consistently drawn throughout.

dimensions.name: Evidence type accuracy
dimensions.rationale: Evidence types correctly assigned throughout. Direct evidence for stated facts (age, birthplace, name, occupation) from the census record. Indirect evidence for computed birth years ('born approximately 1843... inferred by researcher from reported age'). For relationships in the 1850 census (no explicit relationship column), relationship assertions correctly marked indirect with clear rationale: '1850 census has no relationship column; relationship inferred from household position and shared surname Flynn. Introduced in 1880 census only.' Residence marked direct (enumerator observed it). All classifications align with source content and census-era structure.

aggregated_dimensions.name: Correctness
aggregated_dimensions.rationale: The skill correctly extracted 24 assertions from the 1860 census record covering all four persons (Silas Kerrigan, Thomas Flynn, Mary Flynn, Patrick Flynn). Assertions accurately reflect the source data: names, ages, birthplaces, occupations (for Silas and Thomas only), and residence. Evidence types are correctly classified as direct/indirect per 1860 census structure (no relationship column). Informants are properly identified as unknown household members for name/age/birthplace facts. The FAN lead about Silas Kerrigan is surfaced appropriately as a hypothesis, not a fabricated assertion. File writes succeeded and assertions were persisted correctly.

aggregated_dimensions.name: Completeness
aggregated_dimensions.rationale: All four household members were extracted with atomic assertions per fact type. For each person: name, age, birth year (inferred), birthplace, and residence were captured. Occupations correctly extracted only for Silas (laborer) and Thomas (miner), and omitted for Mary and Patrick as required. Relationships inferred from household position (Thomas–Mary as couple, Patrick as their child) were included with indirect evidence type. The FAN lead about Kerrigan kinship was addressed. Source and log entries were created. No required data was omitted.

aggregated_dimensions.name: Tool Arguments
aggregated_dimensions.rationale: MCP tool calls were appropriate. Log append call captured the census record with correct query fields (record_type, year, location, persons). Tree edit to add source completed successfully. Research append calls persisted source description and 24 assertions in proper structure. The second research_append call initially failed but was retried after creating the source via tree_edit, correcting the source ID mismatch. All critical identifiers (source IDs, fact types, record roles) match the expected schema. Extra biographical fields in assertions (structured_value, informant_bias_notes) are reasonable documentation additions, not noise.

aggregated_dimensions.name: Assertion atomicity
aggregated_dimensions.rationale: Each assertion captures a single extractable fact. Name, age, birth year, birthplace, occupation, and residence are separated into distinct `a_` entries with individual `fact_type` fields. Compound information (e.g., estimated birth year from age + census year) is decomposed into separate assertions: age is stated directly, birth is inferred with `evidence_type: indirect`. Relationship inferences (spouse, child) are isolated as separate Relationship assertions. No single assertion conflates multiple facts—all are atomically structured for downstream querying.

aggregated_dimensions.name: Informant identification
aggregated_dimensions.rationale: The skill correctly distinguishes informants from the census enumerator (recorder). For name/age/birthplace facts, informants are identified as 'unknown household member' (e.g., 'likely Thomas Flynn himself' or 'likely a parent' for Patrick). Informant proximity is set to `household_member` for facts requiring personal knowledge, and `witness` for residence (enumerator-observed). For residence assertions, the census enumerator is correctly identified as the informant. The distinction between informant and recorder is explicit in informant_bias_notes, recognizing that adults typically self-reported or a household member provided information.

aggregated_dimensions.name: Evidence type accuracy
aggregated_dimensions.rationale: Evidence types correctly reflect the 1860 census structure. Direct evidence is assigned to facts explicitly stated in the record: name, age, birthplace, occupation. Indirect evidence is assigned to derived facts: birth year (estimated by subtracting age from census year; record states age, not birth year). Relationships (spouse and child) are marked indirect because the 1860 census had no relationship column—the inferences rest on household position and enumeration order, not explicit statements. The skill correctly notes that pre-1880 census relationships are always inferred and explains the basis (matching surnames, consecutive enumeration, age patterns). All evidence type assignments align with the actual content and structure of the source.

**Extraction complete — 1860 U.S. Federal Census, Schuylkill County, PA**

- **Source:** `src_001` / `S1` — original; user-provided transcription of the 1860 federal census population schedule
- **Assertions:** 24 total (`a_001`–`a_024`)
  - `head_of_household` (Silas Kerrigan): 6 — name, age, birth ~1798, birthplace Ireland, occupation laborer, residence
  - `boarder_1` (Thomas Flynn): 7 — name, age, birth ~1818, birthplace Ireland, occupation miner, residence, inferred-spouse relationship
  - `boarder_2` (Mary Flynn): 5 — name, age, birth ~1822, birthplace Ireland, residence
  - `child_1` (Patrick Flynn / subject I1): 6 — name, age, birth ~1845, birthplace Pennsylvania, residence, inferred-child relationship
- **Tree changes:** `S1` source description added; no sibling stubs (no other children in household; Thomas and Mary Flynn not yet in tree, so `ParentChild` edges are out of scope for this pass)
- **Key finding:** Patrick Flynn (age 15, born Pennsylvania) is enumerated with Thomas Flynn (42, Ireland, miner) and Mary Flynn (38, Ireland) — household position is consistent with Patrick being their son. Both are strong candidates for his parents (addresses the project objective)
- **FAN lead:** Silas Kerrigan (head, age 62, Ireland) heads the household in which the Flynn family lives. This could indicate kinship — possibly a parent, sibling, or in-law of Thomas or Mary Flynn — and may surface a maiden name or immigrant connection; flag for `hypothesis-tracking`
- **Next steps:** Run `person-evidence` to link `child_1` assertions to subject `I1`; then `check-warnings` for plausibility; then `assertion-classification` to refine the indeterminate information-quality ratings

dimensions.name: Correctness
dimensions.rationale: The skill correctly extracted 24 assertions from the 1860 census record covering all four persons (Silas Kerrigan, Thomas Flynn, Mary Flynn, Patrick Flynn). Assertions accurately reflect the source data: names, ages, birthplaces, occupations (for Silas and Thomas only), and residence. Evidence types are correctly classified as direct/indirect per 1860 census structure (no relationship column). Informants are properly identified as unknown household members for name/age/birthplace facts. The FAN lead about Silas Kerrigan is surfaced appropriately as a hypothesis, not a fabricated assertion. File writes succeeded and assertions were persisted correctly.

dimensions.name: Completeness
dimensions.rationale: All four household members were extracted with atomic assertions per fact type. For each person: name, age, birth year (inferred), birthplace, and residence were captured. Occupations correctly extracted only for Silas (laborer) and Thomas (miner), and omitted for Mary and Patrick as required. Relationships inferred from household position (Thomas–Mary as couple, Patrick as their child) were included with indirect evidence type. The FAN lead about Kerrigan kinship was addressed. Source and log entries were created. No required data was omitted.

dimensions.name: Tool Arguments
dimensions.rationale: MCP tool calls were appropriate. Log append call captured the census record with correct query fields (record_type, year, location, persons). Tree edit to add source completed successfully. Research append calls persisted source description and 24 assertions in proper structure. The second research_append call initially failed but was retried after creating the source via tree_edit, correcting the source ID mismatch. All critical identifiers (source IDs, fact types, record roles) match the expected schema. Extra biographical fields in assertions (structured_value, informant_bias_notes) are reasonable documentation additions, not noise.

dimensions.name: Assertion atomicity
dimensions.rationale: Each assertion captures a single extractable fact. Name, age, birth year, birthplace, occupation, and residence are separated into distinct `a_` entries with individual `fact_type` fields. Compound information (e.g., estimated birth year from age + census year) is decomposed into separate assertions: age is stated directly, birth is inferred with `evidence_type: indirect`. Relationship inferences (spouse, child) are isolated as separate Relationship assertions. No single assertion conflates multiple facts—all are atomically structured for downstream querying.

dimensions.name: Informant identification
dimensions.rationale: The skill correctly distinguishes informants from the census enumerator (recorder). For name/age/birthplace facts, informants are identified as 'unknown household member' (e.g., 'likely Thomas Flynn himself' or 'likely a parent' for Patrick). Informant proximity is set to `household_member` for facts requiring personal knowledge, and `witness` for residence (enumerator-observed). For residence assertions, the census enumerator is correctly identified as the informant. The distinction between informant and recorder is explicit in informant_bias_notes, recognizing that adults typically self-reported or a household member provided information.

dimensions.name: Evidence type accuracy
dimensions.rationale: Evidence types correctly reflect the 1860 census structure. Direct evidence is assigned to facts explicitly stated in the record: name, age, birthplace, occupation. Indirect evidence is assigned to derived facts: birth year (estimated by subtracting age from census year; record states age, not birth year). Relationships (spouse and child) are marked indirect because the 1860 census had no relationship column—the inferences rest on household position and enumeration order, not explicit statements. The skill correctly notes that pre-1880 census relationships are always inferred and explains the basis (matching surnames, consecutive enumeration, age patterns). All evidence type assignments align with the actual content and structure of the source.

aggregated_dimensions.name: Correctness
aggregated_dimensions.rationale: The skill extracted all facts from the marriage record accurately. Facts about both groom and bride are correctly stated: Patrick Flynn (age 25, Ireland-born, coal miner, parents Thomas Flynn and Mary Brennan) and Catherine Gallagher (age 22, Pennsylvania-born, parents James Gallagher and Bridget Dolan). Marriage date, place, and officiant are correctly captured. The skill correctly identified and flagged the birthplace conflict (Ireland vs. project's Pennsylvania expectation) with proper documentation. All 21 assertions are factually supported by the record text provided.

aggregated_dimensions.name: Completeness
aggregated_dimensions.rationale: The skill comprehensively extracted all required information from the marriage record. It created assertions for both groom and bride with complete fact sets (name, age, birthplace, residence, occupation, marriage event, and parent relationships). Parent names were extracted for both parties. Witnesses (Thomas Flynn and James Gallagher) were noted with informant_bias_notes explaining their dual roles. The research log and source citation were properly documented. No extractable information from the record was omitted.

aggregated_dimensions.name: Tool Arguments
aggregated_dimensions.rationale: Three MCP tool calls were made. The first two calls to research_log_append and research_append had correct arguments semantically (query description, outcome, section names, operation types). However, the second research_append call failed with a validation error ('S1' not found in tree.gedcomx.json sources) because the skill attempted to reference S1 before it was created. The skill then correctly called tree_edit to add_source (which generated S1), then re-attempted research_append successfully. While the workflow recovered correctly, the initial argument ordering (referencing S1 before creation) represents a sequencing error that had to be corrected.

aggregated_dimensions.name: Assertion atomicity
aggregated_dimensions.rationale: Each assertion represents a single extractable fact. Names are separate from ages, births, residences. Birth dates and birthplaces are split across distinct assertions (a_003 for computed birth year, a_004 for birthplace). Marriage assertions include date and place but these are correctly grouped as one marriage event (date, location, spouse, officiant are components of a single marriage fact). Parent relationships are atomic (father and mother in separate assertions). Compound information has been properly decomposed into individual fact_type entries querying separately.

aggregated_dimensions.name: Informant identification
aggregated_dimensions.rationale: Informants are correctly identified with proper proximity designations. For groom's own facts (name, age, birthplace, residence, occupation, parent relationships), informant is 'Patrick Flynn (groom)' with proximity 'self'. For bride's own facts, informant is 'Catherine Gallagher (bride)' with proximity 'self'. For parent names (Thomas Flynn, Mary Brennan, James Gallagher, Bridget Dolan), informants are the respective children with proximity 'family_not_present' (since parents didn't provide the information directly). Marriage event informant is 'Rev. Daniel O'Connor (officiant)' with proximity 'official_duty'. The distinction between recorder (clerk) and actual informants is properly maintained.

aggregated_dimensions.name: Evidence type accuracy
aggregated_dimensions.rationale: Evidence types are correctly assigned. Direct evidence: name, age, occupation, residence, birthplace, marriage date/place, and all explicitly stated parent names (all marked 'direct' as they appear on the record). Indirect evidence: birth years computed from stated ages (~1845 from age 25, ~1848 from age 22) are correctly marked 'indirect' with informant_bias_notes explaining the arithmetic inference. The marriage event (date, place, officiant) is direct evidence as it's explicitly stated on the record. All relationship assertions about parents are direct because the parties explicitly stated their parents' names on the marriage license application.

Source entry properly documents the 1850 Census. Evidence-type classifications are accurate (direct for stated facts, indirect for inferred birth year and relationship).

dimensions.name: Completeness

Extracted the ParentChild relationship as indirect evidence. Created source entry pointing to United States Census 1850 collection with full FamilySearch URL. Logged the search. All expectations from the per-test context were met.

dimensions.name: Tool Arguments
dimensions.rationale: All tool calls passed correct arguments. record_read received recordId 'ark:/61903/1:1:68Q9-K34P' matching expected ~68Q9-K34P pattern. research_log_append and research_append received correct projectPath and section parameters. tree_edit received proper operation and source parameters. After initial research_append failed due to missing S1 in gedcomx, skill recovered by calling tree_edit to create the source first, then re-invoked research_append successfully—correct error recovery workflow.

dimensions.name: Assertion atomicity
dimensions.rationale: Each assertion is a single extractable fact. Patrick's data decomposed into five separate assertions: name (a_001), birth year (a_002), birthplace (a_003), residence (a_004), relationship (a_005). Thomas's data into three: name (a_006), residence (a_007), relationship (a_008). Compound information from record_read (e.g., birth fact containing both date and place in response) was properly split into separate fact_type entries. No assertions conflate multiple facts.

dimensions.name: Informant identification
dimensions.rationale: Skill correctly distinguished informant from recorder. For Patrick's name, birth date, and birthplace: 'unknown household member (likely a parent)' with proximity 'household_member'—acknowledging the informant is a household resident, not the enumerator. For residence: 'census enumerator' with proximity 'witness'—correct classification of the recorder as witness. For Thomas (head of household): 'unknown household member (likely self or spouse)'—appropriate given his household position. Reasoning provided in informant_bias_notes where needed.

dimensions.name: Evidence type accuracy
dimensions.rationale: Evidence types correctly assigned per source. Patrick's name, birthplace ('Ireland' stated in record) marked direct evidence—correctly reflects explicit record content. Birth year computed from 1850 census age marked indirect—appropriately noted in value and reasoning. ParentChild relationship marked indirect for both Patrick and Thomas—correct classification since 1850 census has no relationship column and relationship is inferred from household position by indexer, not explicitly stated. Skill demonstrated understanding of pre-1880 census limitations and direct vs. inferred evidence.

output.text_response:
I'll kick off the assertion extraction and load the FamilySearch matches tool schema at the same time.Good. `research.json` is empty (no sources, no log), and `tree.gedcomx.json` has only I1 = Patrick Flynn. Now I'll run the extraction.

**Record analysis:**
- Source: 1850 U.S. Federal Census — **original** (schedules are first-hand recordings)
- Roles: Thomas Flynn = `head_of_household` (record title only; not in gedcomx → no persona ID for assertions), Patrick Flynn = `child_1` (age ~5, same surname, household position)
- 5d trigger: Patrick is child_1 ✓ but Thomas Flynn is **not** in `tree.gedcomx.json` → skip sibling stubs
- Place standard values: "Ireland" and "Branch Township, Schuylkill, Pennsylvania" taken directly from the gedcomx (record_search-resolved)

Step 4 first — create the log entry to get its ID:Log `log_001` created. Now the batched source + assertions write:Two fixes needed:
1. `S1` must exist in `tree.gedcomx.json` before `research.json` can reference it — write the tree source first.
2. `user_provided` log entries carry no sidecar, so `record_persona_id` must be `null` on all assertions.`S1` written. Now the batched source + 6 assertions, with `record_persona_id: null` throughout:All 7 entries written cleanly. Now checking FamilySearch record-record matches for persona MXHY-TP4:All done. Here's the terse summary:

---

**Extraction complete**

- **Source:** `src_001` / `S1` — 1850 U.S. Federal Census, original, FamilySearch
- **Assertions:** 6 for `child_1` (Patrick Flynn, P1) — a_001–a_006
  - name, age (direct); birth year (indirect, ~1845 from age 5); birthplace Ireland (direct); residence Branch Twp., Schuylkill, PA (direct); household-position relationship (indirect)
- **Tree:** `S1` source description added; no sibling stubs (Thomas Flynn not in tree, so 5d trigger skipped)
- **Key finding:** Thomas Flynn (`head_of_household`, same surname) is a **high-priority FAN lead / probable father** — not yet in the tree. Assertions for him omitted (no gedcomx persona ID; sidecar absent). Recommend a live `record_read` on MXHY-TP4 to retrieve his full persona for separate extraction.
- **record_persona_id:** set to `null` on all assertions — `user_provided` log entry carries no sidecar; person-evidence will need to pass `record_persona_id: "P1"` manually when calling `same_person`.

**FamilySearch record-record matches (informational only):**
| Confidence | Record | Status |
|---|---|---|
| **4** (≥ threshold) | 1860 U.S. Federal Census (MABC) | pending |
| 3 | Pennsylvania Death Certificates 1906–1967 (MDEF) | pending |

The 1860 census match (confidence 4, score 0.88) is worth pursuing next — it would give a 10-year follow-up on Patrick in Schuylkill County and potentially name Thomas Flynn again as head, helping confirm the father hypothesis.

**Next steps:** `person-evidence` to link a_001–a_006 → I1, then `search-records` for the 1860 census match (MABC).

warnings.advisory: Skill called MCP tools but the rubric has no dimension name suggesting tool-usage coverage (matched against keywords: ['tool usage', 'tool use', 'tool work', 'tool call', 'argument quality', 'argument-quality', 'response interpretation', 'tool selection', 'mcp tool', 'fixture']). The judge will grade other dimensions but won't score tool work explicitly. Consider adding a tool-usage dimension or renaming an existing one.

dimensions.name: Correctness
dimensions.rationale: All assertions extracted from the 1850 census record are factually accurate and properly supported by the gedcomx data provided. The 6 assertions (name, age, birth year, birthplace, residence, relationship) correctly reflect the source content. The skill correctly identified evidence types: direct for explicitly stated facts (name, age, birthplace, residence), indirect for the derived birth year and household-position relationship. The FamilySearch record-record matches (confidence 4 for 1860 census, confidence 3 for Pennsylvania Death Certificates) were accurately reported from the tool response. No fabricated material.

dimensions.name: Completeness
dimensions.rationale: The skill completed all required work: (1) extracted 6 assertions from the 1850 census for Patrick Flynn; (2) identified Thomas Flynn as head of household (high-priority FAN lead and probable father); (3) called record_record_matches with the correct id MXHY-TP4; (4) reported both pending collateral matches (1860 census with confidence 4, Pennsylvania Death Certificates with confidence 3); (5) highlighted the 1860 census as higher-priority. All expected behaviors from the test context were addressed.

dimensions.name: Tool Arguments
dimensions.rationale: The critical tool call—record_record_matches with id 'MXHY-TP4'—matched the expected_args exactly. The research_log_append and tree_edit calls were procedurally correct. The research_append calls properly corrected the initial error by setting record_persona_id to null (appropriate for user_provided log entries without a sidecar). All identifier fields and critical parameters were correct.

dimensions.name: Assertion atomicity
dimensions.rationale: Each of the 6 assertions is a single, atomic fact. Name, age, birth year, birthplace, residence, and relationship are each extracted as separate entries with their own fact_type. Compound information (e.g., 'Patrick Flynn, age 5, born Ireland') was properly decomposed into individual assertions. Each assertion carries one extractable fact with its own fact_type, allowing downstream skills to query individual facts.

dimensions.name: Informant identification
dimensions.rationale: Informant identification is precise and well-reasoned. For name/age/birthplace (child's facts), the skill used 'unknown household member (likely a parent)' with proximity 'household_member', supported by informant_bias_notes explaining the reasoning (e.g., 'Patrick was approximately 5 years old in 1850; a parent likely provided his name to the enumerator'). For residence, correctly used 'census enumerator' with proximity 'witness'. The distinction between recorder (enumerator) and actual informant (household member) is properly maintained throughout.

dimensions.name: Evidence type accuracy
dimensions.rationale: Evidence types are correctly assigned for all assertions. Direct evidence: name, age, birthplace, residence (all explicitly stated in the census). Indirect evidence: birth year (derived arithmetically from age 5 in 1850, not explicitly recorded) and relationship (inferred from household position and surname match, as the 1850 census has no explicit relationship column). The skill correctly recognized this 1850-specific constraint and did not treat household position as direct evidence.

aggregated_dimensions.name: Correctness
aggregated_dimensions.rationale: Most assertions are correctly extracted and cited to log_001, and record_persona_id values (P1, P2, P3) match the gedcomx. However, one critical issue: birth year 1845 is marked as direct evidence when it is actually indirect—computed from age 5 in the 1850 census, not stated directly in the source. This mislabeling undermines genealogical reasoning, as a downstream skill cannot trust the evidence classification for conflict resolution.

aggregated_dimensions.name: Completeness
aggregated_dimensions.rationale: All extractable assertions from the record were produced: Patrick (P1) yielded 7 assertions (name, age, computed birth year, birthplace, residence, 2× relationship), Thomas (P2) yielded 2 (name, residence), and Mary (P3) yielded 2 (name, residence). The skill correctly included parent-child relationships as indirect evidence and skipped the sibling-stub rule per instructions. All 11 assertions accounted for, and the source entry correctly references log_001.

aggregated_dimensions.name: Tool Arguments
aggregated_dimensions.rationale: Both MCP calls (tree_edit add_source and research_append) executed with correct args. The arkUrl was copied verbatim in full form, source title matched the record, log_entry_id 'log_001' was correctly referenced instead of creating a new entry, and all 11 assertion entries were appended with proper record_persona_id values (P1, P2, P3) matching the gedcomx person IDs. No fixture errors.

aggregated_dimensions.name: Assertion atomicity
aggregated_dimensions.rationale: Every assertion is a single extractable fact. Name, age, birth year, birthplace, and residence are each separate assertions with their own fact_type. The two relationship assertions are distinct entries, one per inferred parent. Compound information (e.g., place and date in residence assertions) are properly decomposed into separate structured_value fields. Downstream skills can query individual facts without ambiguity.

aggregated_dimensions.name: Informant identification
aggregated_dimensions.rationale: Informant identification is explicit and accurate. For Patrick's name, age, birth year, and birthplace, the skill uses 'unknown household member (likely a parent)' with household_member proximity and clear reasoning in informant_bias_notes (e.g., 'A child aged approximately 5 could not self-report'). For Thomas and Mary's name, the skill correctly identifies 'unknown household member (likely self or spouse)'. For residence facts, 'census enumerator' with witness proximity is used. The skill properly distinguishes recorder from informant across roles.

aggregated_dimensions.name: Evidence type accuracy
aggregated_dimensions.rationale: Most evidence types are correct: relationship assertions are properly marked indirect (1850 census has no relationship column), residence facts are direct (enumerator witness), and name/birthplace for the child are direct (stated in record). However, the computed birth year (1845 from age 5 in 1850) is incorrectly marked as direct when it is indirect—it is arithmetic inference, not a direct statement in the source. This mislabeling on one of seven child assertions impacts genealogical reasoning reliability for conflict resolution downstream.

output.text_response:
**State check:** `research.json` — sources `[]`, assertions `[]`, log has `log_001` (record_search, positive). `tree.gedcomx.json` — one person I1 Patrick Flynn, no sources.

**Plan:**
- Source → `src_001` / `S1` (1850 census, original)
- Roles: P1 Patrick = `child_1`, P2 Thomas = `head_of_household`, P3 Mary = `wife`
- Assertions: 7 for child_1 (name, age-direct, birth-year-indirect, birthplace, residence, 2× relationship-inferred), 2 for head (name, residence), 2 for wife (name, residence) = 11 total
- No siblings on record → sibling-stub rule skipped
- Log entry already exists (`log_001`) → no new log entryThe tree write succeeded (S1 assigned). The `research_append` batch rejected because the schema still requires top-level `section`+`op` even when `ops` is supplied — retrying with both present:All writes confirmed valid. Summary:

- **Source:** `src_001` / `S1` — 1850 U.S. Federal Census, original, FamilySearch
- **Assertions:** 11 total
  - `child_1` (Patrick, P1): a_001–a_007 — name, age (direct), birth year ~1845 (indirect, computed from age), birthplace Ireland, residence, 2× parent relationship (indirect, no 1880 relationship column)
  - `head_of_household` (Thomas, P2): a_008–a_009 — name, residence
  - `wife` (Mary, P3): a_010–a_011 — name, residence
- **Tree:** S1 added; no sibling stubs (no siblings on record)
- **Key finding:** Patrick's Irish birthplace is direct but parent-reported (indeterminate quality); both Thomas Flynn and Mary Flynn implicated as parents via household position only — both warrant person-evidence investigation

**Next:** run `check-warnings` on I1, then `person-evidence` to link P1 → I1 and create tree stubs for Thomas (P2) and Mary (P3).

dimensions.name: Correctness
dimensions.rationale: Most assertions are correctly extracted and cited to log_001, and record_persona_id values (P1, P2, P3) match the gedcomx. However, one critical issue: birth year 1845 is marked as direct evidence when it is actually indirect—computed from age 5 in the 1850 census, not stated directly in the source. This mislabeling undermines genealogical reasoning, as a downstream skill cannot trust the evidence classification for conflict resolution.

dimensions.name: Completeness
dimensions.rationale: All extractable assertions from the record were produced: Patrick (P1) yielded 7 assertions (name, age, computed birth year, birthplace, residence, 2× relationship), Thomas (P2) yielded 2 (name, residence), and Mary (P3) yielded 2 (name, residence). The skill correctly included parent-child relationships as indirect evidence and skipped the sibling-stub rule per instructions. All 11 assertions accounted for, and the source entry correctly references log_001.

dimensions.name: Tool Arguments
dimensions.rationale: Both MCP calls (tree_edit add_source and research_append) executed with correct args. The arkUrl was copied verbatim in full form, source title matched the record, log_entry_id 'log_001' was correctly referenced instead of creating a new entry, and all 11 assertion entries were appended with proper record_persona_id values (P1, P2, P3) matching the gedcomx person IDs. No fixture errors.

dimensions.name: Assertion atomicity
dimensions.rationale: Every assertion is a single extractable fact. Name, age, birth year, birthplace, and residence are each separate assertions with their own fact_type. The two relationship assertions are distinct entries, one per inferred parent. Compound information (e.g., place and date in residence assertions) are properly decomposed into separate structured_value fields. Downstream skills can query individual facts without ambiguity.

dimensions.name: Informant identification
dimensions.rationale: Informant identification is explicit and accurate. For Patrick's name, age, birth year, and birthplace, the skill uses 'unknown household member (likely a parent)' with household_member proximity and clear reasoning in informant_bias_notes (e.g., 'A child aged approximately 5 could not self-report'). For Thomas and Mary's name, the skill correctly identifies 'unknown household member (likely self or spouse)'. For residence facts, 'census enumerator' with witness proximity is used. The skill properly distinguishes recorder from informant across roles.

dimensions.name: Evidence type accuracy
dimensions.rationale: Most evidence types are correct: relationship assertions are properly marked indirect (1850 census has no relationship column), residence facts are direct (enumerator witness), and name/birthplace for the child are direct (stated in record). However, the computed birth year (1845 from age 5 in 1850) is incorrectly marked as direct when it is indirect—it is arithmetic inference, not a direct statement in the source. This mislabeling on one of seven child assertions impacts genealogical reasoning reliability for conflict resolution downstream.
